speaker: interviewee
text: At home, they called me [? Zoli ?] Schwimmer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here on in America, from [? Zoli ?] became Sydney.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sydney.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Dolha, a small little town in the Carpathian Mountains. It was, they call it-- it's a crossroad town. Because from that little small town, you can go different directions. You can go one direction to Beregsaz, which a larger town. You can go to Khust. You can go to Munkacs, [INAUDIBLE]. Or you can go to Svalyava. That's why from other smaller, rural areas used to come to Dolha. And from there, they go. I'm sure you'll find it on a map. I'm going to talk a lot about Dolha, because there where I was born, and there I grew up. And it was my home. Now, when I was born, it was Austria-Hungary. I was born in 1915, December the 14. So you can figure out now my age already.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. And I will ask you that, by the way, at the very beginning. I'll say your name and you spell it. And then what was your date of birth? And how older are you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you will say that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I already, I already--

speaker: interviewer
text: When we're really on tape. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: So next--

speaker: interviewer
text: The date is May 6th, 1996. The survivor is Sydney Schwimmer, S-Y-D-N-E-Y S-C-H-W-I-M-M-E-R. The interviewer is Joan Baker. The interview is being conducted in Miami Beach, Florida, in the United States. It's being conducted in English. And this is tape number one. My name is Joan Baker, J-O-A-N B-A-K-E-R. The date is May 6, 1996. I'm interviewing Sydney Schwimmer in Miami Beach, Florida. And the interview is being conducted in English. Would you give us your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Sydney Schwimmer. Spell it?

speaker: interviewer
text: And would you spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-C-H-W-I-M-M-E-R. Actually, they called me at home [? Zoli ?] Schwimmer. When I was born, it was Austria Hungry. And in those days, they give Hungarian names. My Hebrew name is Zalman Schwimmer. But now in America, we use it Sydney Schwimmer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born December 14, 1915. Now we can figure out my age.

speaker: interviewer
text: You are 80 years old now.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in December 80 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Could you, could you tell us first about your hometown? Describe the hometown and especially the relationship of the Jewish and non-Jewish population? What was your hometown like?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Dolha. This was a small town in the Carpathian Mountains. It belonged, once upon a time when I was born, to Austria-Hungary. Later, from 1918 till 1939, it was Czechoslovakia, next as, as [? Carpatho-Russia, ?] Russia. From 1939 till 1945, it was Hungary, next to Hungary. Later on, it the Soviet-occupied, Soviet territories. And now it's in Ukraine, Dolha now. The spelling of Dolha has different spelling. Actually, they call it Dolha, however in the Russian it is Dovhoje or how they spell it there is Dolgoye. So if you look somewhere places, you may find under the name Dolha, Dolgoye or Dovhoje. Now, this is small little town. It's important because has a crossroads. In other words, from that little town, from Dolha, you can go three different direction to larger cities, like Khust, Beregsaz, Munkacs, or even to Svalyava.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the population of Dolha?

speaker: interviewee
text: The population in Dolha is the following. In 1944, there were approximately 110 Jewish families. However, there lived a lot of non-Jewish families, mostly Carpatho-Russians, Ukrainians, and other minorities also. I don't exactly in numbers to say the population. The, the houses were approximately 800. Now, figure out that in every household, there are four, five, six children, so I would say approximately maybe close to 4,000. Today, I don't know how much is there.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Dolha, mostly, most of the thing that lived there were Ukranians, Russians. But they lived there already for centuries. Shall I tell you, when I was born, there was illiteracy approximately 95% percent, if not more, among people-- not among Jews, but among the other. Why? Because they didn't go to school in the Hungarian regime. And even now, during, during the regime of Czechoslovakia, to send a child to school was-- they needed the child for something else or some other purpose, especially the non-Jews. They needed the child to go with the cow or somewhere else.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's only when it was so obligatory that they were punished or something, they were put fines, so they did send the child to a school. Naturally, the school was very primitive. Now, Jewish boys usually-- even girls-- went to a Jewish school they called the cheder. It's a private school like they have here, but it was a whole day. In that school, they learned only Hebrew. Or they learned only mostly religious thing. And so Jewish children also didn't go to school only. But it was obligatory. During the Czech regime, I would say in the early '90s, they made it a point that everyone must go to school. So children started to go a little bit more who grew up. Mostly, they had about four grades. That's about all. The fifth, sixth grade, very seldom did they go. And so they remain.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what was the population there? Mostly, they had little farms, as we call it. But these little farms were very, very small. And they work on their farms, the peasants. And from this, they made their living. Whatever was growing, this how about they lived on it. And hardly did they have for the whole year.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a great poverty. I cannot describe now what poverty means. It really was a poverty that concerns, sometimes, on some occasions, people didn't have what to eat. Now, Jews were also poor. But few of them had some businesses which did a little better. For instance, my father was a tinsmith. He was the only tinsmith in town. So he had a little more income than the rest, but also not talking about something big. We had what to eat, not very-- too much. But we weren't so poor in a sense. And also, we were in a sense that we could help out some other people also. This was normal. People didn't go to look for something else. People didn't go look for work. There was nowhere to work. And that's how people lived there for centuries and so on. Actually--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were in your family, when you talk about your father supporting a family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, now, my father's name was Jacob Schwimmer. My father was also born, not in Dolha, nearby, a few kilometer, by a place where they call it [INAUDIBLE]. It's on the map. And my father lived there almost-- when he married my mother-- since he was 24 and lived all those years. My mother's name was Grunwald, Celia. She was also from the nearby town. As far as our family is concerned, we had-- my mother had more children, but only five have survived. I mean, there were sicknesses and illnesses over there. People used to have eight and ten children. If four or five survived, it was very good.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were five children. I had a sister. My older sister, Helene was born in 1908. Then I had a brother, Abraham, was born in 1910. Then I came in 1915. Then I have another brother, which is [? Moshe, ?] which was born in 1918, and another sister which was born in 1919. Her name is, was Dvora.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, from the entire family, from ours, only the three brothers remained alive. One of my brother died recently. My older sister, Helene, who was married and had two children, perished in the Holocaust in Auschwitz. She was married to a man by the name of Weinberger in Beregsaz. She was about 36, 37 years old. She had a boy of about 13 and a girl about seven or eight. Entire family perished in Auschwitz. No one survived. Her husband was working in Auschwitz and later on in Wolfsburg. But also, he didn't survive, so completely the entire family. My two brothers survived-- my older and my younger-- because they were not in Auschwitz. At the time, when they took Jews to Auschwitz they were not [INAUDIBLE]. They were labor camps, so they survived at that early age when Dolha or some other places were liberated. My youngest sister, she once went to Auschwitz with the entire family. She was working. But on a certain-- she was working in a factory, where they made [BACKGROUND NOISE] munitions somewhere in Germany. And so she did not survive. There was a bombardment around 1944, in-- around September. And hundreds and hundreds of girls were there killed. English bombarded this munition factory. She was killed there. And then we had uncles and aunts, numerous, not to talk, but can describe each one. Very few survived. My father has a brother by the name of Hersh Schwimmer. Entire family perished-- three children [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, did that extended family live around your community?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. They lived also in Dolha. And also, they-- from my mother's side, many, many of her, none of those survived-- very, very few, one here and there. Cannot enumerate right now, because there is too many to enumerate who, who are survived, who remained alive. But most of them perished in Auschwitz. And most of them perished around the May 18, 19, or 20, depending where they came. And they came 1944. Now coming back to Dolha, I remember almost from my early childhood. Now first, I went to cheder, which they call it a religious school, almost up to 10. But then at age of 10, somehow, my father thought that I should learn something else. Also, despite we learned in religious school some other things also, but he wanted that his children should be a little bit more knowledgeable.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went for one year in public school there and finished fourth grade only. To tell you about the public school the way I remember it, teachers were also very poorly trained. But it wasn't necessary there. It was, you know, corporal punishments in schools, like those days. Needless to say, everyone is familiar.

speaker: interviewee
text: After I-- I went to this school. Now, about this population in Dolha, I'll talk about the relationship between the Jews and the other population. Basically, the people who lived there were friendly. And we lived in harmony with the non-Jews there. Because we depended on each-- on another. And also, the-- the non-Jewish population in Dolha, not only did they depend, but they were friendly. And they didn't know about anti-Semitism. It was very little. It wasn't-- they knew there are Jews there. But it wasn't-- it didn't occur to them. They helped out each other. As I say, they were illiterate, depended a lot on the, on the Jews. Some Jews had stores. On the other hand, there was a population also of other who came like, from Hungary-- they remained from World War I there-- and some others who were a little bit more cultural, they say. They were anti-Semite, but not in a sense that they showed it immediately. Because it was during the regime of Czechoslovakia. And it was democracy, but you could feel them. Naturally, when I'm speaking about anti-Semitism-- I went to school, to this public school-- non-Jewish boys were throwing stones, not because they knew what anti-Semitism is. It was for a simple reason. It was a Jew. And somehow, for a simple reason, they, they didn't mix. They didn't mingle. But there were no-- basically no anti-Semites, in a sense. They didn't know what it is. It is true that in some communities, there were more anti-Semitism for a simple reason because in the churches, the priests, even they were lenient. But they were preaching that the Jews killed Christ. Now, the little boy who was 10 or 11 years old heard this in the church, Jews killed Christ. So probably this was the basis of being them somehow anti-Semite to a Jew, because the Jew killed him. That's how it went. But nothing-- for no other reason were they anti-Semite. As I said, but the more intelligent who were living there and who were like, from Hungary, Hungarians, so they were [INAUDIBLE] warned, they were somehow anti-Semites, but secretly. During the Czech regime was no anti-Semites in that sense. Jews occupied themselves mostly with some trade. Some were small, little farmers. And there a few who had there some business, but not talking about-- grocery business, but not necessarily like today's supermarkets, had stores for basic necessities in town, like kerosene, and soap, or needles, for basic things.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about religious observance? How was the Jewish community--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, religious observance-- religious observance, actually, almost in the whole Carpathian, people were all the religions. There was no such a thing as non-religious. Everyone not to go to a synagogue, who heard that they counted them, they knew exactly who goes, and almost every one. But was nothing else to be. This was the only place like, we'll say, for entertainment. Not-- they didn't go for entertainment. But it is the only place for-- where they went for-- to, to synagogue. We had a rabbi, a very distinguished man by the name of Usher Zelig Greenshwieg. He was an old man, but one of the most great scholars. But his-- he distinguished himself. He was a person who loved every one, who was a person who couldn't hate everyone. And can you imagine a man-- he was almost 80 years old-- if he went on the street and he saw a non-Jew, even a girl, he would bow her-- his head. He was a great, great man and had pity and mercy for everyone, regardless of the-- of who. As a matter of fact, I'll tell a small, little story. Usually, people used to quarrel and came to courts or something. But they came to him to straighten out. And even non- Jew with a Jew who have some quarrel came. They said, let's go to the rabbi, what the rabbi is going to say. And he was so straight and so honest, they believed in him. And if the non-peasant or non-Jew was right, he gave them right. And there was no question. They believed in him. He was a man, a righteous man. Too much to talk about him in this particular time. His name was Usher Zelig Greenshwieg. Talk to him later when we'll come to Auschwitz. Now, come back to my--

speaker: interviewee
text: But he was-- But he was really respected by the entire community.

speaker: interviewee
text: Respected, a respected man by every one-- a beloved man and a respected man because of his humility, and of his honesty, and of his-- everyone respected him in town-- and of his kindness. And not only kind, he was kind to every person-- to a child, to every, to every person. Even another little, small story about him, just to make it-- when we were examined by the rabbi during after a week, we learned whatever, we came to him. And our teacher has examined in front and interviewed everyone. And some boy, for some reason, didn't know the answer, he would whisper him the answer, not to make him ashamed. This was the type of man who was so honest. Coming back, so I finished fourth grade. My father and my mother-- my mother was still alive at that time-- somehow saw that it's a small town. And you cannot go so far. They wanted their children should become something, should have much more education than you can have here in Dolha, a small, little town.

speaker: interviewee
text: So after finishing fourth grade, they did send me to a town, Beregsaz, not far from Dolha. It is about approximately three hours [INAUDIBLE] to train, to study to go there to the gymnasium. In Beregsaz, there were two gymnasiums-- a Hungarian gymnasium and a Russian gymnasium.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Hungarian gymnasium was mostly Hungarian and Jewish also. Because this was a town, 95% Hungarians, talking Hungarian. That's where I learned Hungarian. But since basically I didn't know Hungarian, I couldn't start to go to school there. So my father send me to that school. This was during the Czechoslovakian regime.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a school gymnasium of eight years, eighth, eighth grade. It's, in comparison like here, like, I would say, a high school. And we learned a lot of languages and mathematics, whatever. Now, who were the teachers here? In the Carpathian, very few intellectuals went for a career of being a teacher. And even there were teachers maybe in the elementary school in little town, there was, there was a teacher school in [INAUDIBLE], but after World War I, very few teacher schools. So most of the teachers-- they called them professors in this gymnasium-- most of them-- I would say 90%-- were Ukrainians who actually, during World War I, escaped from Ukraine for some reason-- it was Bolsheviks-- and settled down in Beregsaz. And there was a Russian gymnasium. And they became their teacher.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were intelligent people. They went [INAUDIBLE] in Ukraine. Mostly, they were from Kiev, Odessa. They went to schools. And in the Carpathian, there were no schools. And they were more intelligent and more knowledgeable in history, mathematics, whatever it is. And so we had, most of these teachers, Ukrainians. In the school I went, in the beginning, I think I was the only one Jew in my class. There were later on, some-- were minority, few Jews. Classes were small.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you live with? You were some three hours from your home.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, well, no. The three hours away, I do not want to describe here how poverty was there. My father put me in a place there where a woman had six beds in a room.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like a boarding house.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one bed was [? for nights. ?] He paid, he paid them $3.00. That's all he could afford. And to eat, he had some friends. So I would eat one day here and the other day there, and every dinner in a different place. And I was hungry also, pretty hungry, from a certain occasion. But that's how it was normally there. And coming back, so that's how I was supported. My father, a little bit, somewhat, he could afford. He had more children. He gave me a little money, whatever it was necessary. But he paid that woman. There were six beds in a room. She [? hired them. ?] I mean, not only students-- someone was apprentices. And this was my, my headquarters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And every day, I had another place where I have to go to eat lunch. It wasn't so bad. But some days, I didn't have that to go. So I was hungry, a kid of 11 years old. And so I went to this school. Coming back to these teachers now, these teachers really were anti-Semites. And they couldn't be openly. Because it was Czechoslovakia. There was a regime, because a lot of Jews lived in Beregsaz. But there were giving us books mostly from [INAUDIBLE] and [INAUDIBLE] and all those.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were anti-Semite books. And they themselves, they didn't explicitly make point of it, but a Jew, you know, was to them, it's different. Probably, they came already with this attitude from Ukraine. And they were [INAUDIBLE], you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can name their names, Iljashevich, Zaklinski, Artemovich, Stoklasovo, as I remember them. But it was normally a big deal, anti-Semite [INAUDIBLE] doing. So even, I had to be a good student in order to give for me a good grade. So I really was studying hard and in order to have a grade.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many-- were you the only Jew in your class then?

speaker: interviewee
text: In my class in the beginning, I was the only Jew. Later on was another Jew in other classes, but was-- in the whole gymnasium, was very, very few Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very few.

speaker: interviewee
text: Who are going to the-- to the-- to the Russian gymnasium. Actually, there were a lot of Jews going in school there in the Hungarian gymnasium who were born in Beregsaz. In Beregsaz was a big population several thousand Jews, and also were Hungarians there. This was a completely different story, but we were in the same building. It was a Russian gymnasium. We were speaking Russian. And this was Hungarian gymnasium. This was the place where I learned Hungarian, too, to speak. To mingle among the students also-- so even the priest, who was by the name of [INAUDIBLE]. And he was a priest. He was teaching religion there. I was excused from religion because a different religion. But the priest there also was talking about Christ. I think the priests also were not educated-- that's my opinion-- at a certain point, to a certain degree.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anti-Semitism was there, especially from the, from the teachers. Will I describe you in, in details? No, but this was there. Our principal in the school, he was the principal for the-- for both gymnasium, for the Russian gymnasium and for the Hungarian, was by the name [? Dr. ?] [? Ropole, ?] a Czech, very intelligent person, who knows what democracy was. And he probably kept it so that everybody should even-- he wouldn't let, some discrimination, all right. But you couldn't prevent those teachers in, inside the class not being a little bit hostile, making remarks about Jews, not about person like myself, but about Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so the children or the-- the-- who went there, the students, from-- who were the students also from different places, not from Beregsaz, who are like the children from teachers, from priests. They [INAUDIBLE] also together.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were not anti-Semite, because they didn't understand. And I was-- [? and I were-- ?] time, at that time, an anti-Semite wasn't to be understood like today. It wasn't politically so much. Maybe the Jew is better off. He had the store, so you saw the Jew better off. So he-- the non-Jew was maybe anti-Semite. Why is he better off? But most-- Did you have friends among the non-Jews in this gymnasium?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had-- yeah, I had friends. Because in that school, there were going also some Czech who lived there-- where, where an army base is where there who were Czechs, who were going also to the Russian school, you know? And these were intelligent-- coming from intelligent families. They were not born there. They remained there during because it was an army bases. And their children-- captain's child or a colonel's child-- went to school, by the name of [INAUDIBLE] or [INAUDIBLE]. And they were more like not the peasants from the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you mainly have friends among the Czech students then, rather than the Russian and Hungarian?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, mainly, mainly among-- and I had friends among the Jewish students who went in the Hungarian gymnasium. There were Jews, Jewish boys. And also, non-Jews were there, but Hungarians, you know? [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I wouldn't say so much that anti-Semitism was [INAUDIBLE] Russian gymnasium. You couldn't feel anti-Semitism. No, it wasn't so. However, the professor that teachers in the Hungarian gymnasiums, they were different. They were from pre-World War I. They were there for a long, long time.

speaker: interviewee
text: The gymnasium existed since 1900, probably. There were all the people. There were Hungarians. Some of them were Jews, even teachers. And they were already living in Beregsaz for years. They had their houses. They had their families. And they were more or less settled, I don't know, inside [INAUDIBLE]. But here in our school were mostly who came from Ukraine after World War I. And they settled down because they ran away from the Bolsheviks.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you at this gymnasium?

speaker: interviewee
text: So, this gymnasium is about eight years. Now, what we learned at the gymnasium, mostly languages-- Latin, French, and so on-- all the subject. I spent that eight years until already know something-- I started there, was 11 years old around-- I was 19 years old. Here it comes. This was during the regime of Czechoslovakia. Everyone who was 19 years old during the regime of Czechoslovakia had to go before a draft board. Before I graduated in 1936, I had to go before a draft board to be drafted in the army. However, all who were students and continued their education anyplace else, they were exempt from going to the army. A postponement was given to them. They were drafted. And so I was nine-- approximately 1936, I was almost 19 years. I was also drafted to the Czech army, like everyone else. But I was given-- that I'm going to study further, I was given an exemption as long as I will study.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We need to stop here at the end of the first tape. We'll take that up on the second tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK?
